summary: Implement core calculator math operations (add, subtract, multiply, divide).
description: Add a calculator module with four basic binary operations: add, subtract, multiply, and divide. Each function should accept two numeric arguments and return a number. Handle division by zero by returning a defined value or throwing a clear error. Use plain functions or a small API suitable for the rest of the codebase.
objective: Deliver a minimal, correct set of math operations that the rest of the calculator (or callers) can rely on.
requirements: Implement add(a, b) returning a + b.
Implement subtract(a, b) returning a - b.
Implement multiply(a, b) returning a * b.
Implement divide(a, b) with explicit handling when b is 0 (e.g. throw or return NaN/Infinity).
All operations accept and return numbers (or document type coercion).
constraints: No external math libraries required; use language primitives only.
Keep functions pure and side-effect free where possible.
API must be easy to test in isolation.

summary: Expose calculator operations via a single public API or module.
description: Define the public surface for the calculator: either a single object/namespace exporting add, subtract, multiply, divide, or a module that re-exports these functions. Document usage (e.g. JSDoc or README) and ensure the API is consistent with the rest of the project's patterns (e.g. ESM vs CJS, naming).
objective: Provide a clear, stable entry point for consumers of the calculator (UI, CLI, or other services).
requirements: Single entry point (e.g. index.ts or calculator.ts) exporting all operations.
Public API documented (signatures and behavior, including division-by-zero).
Naming and module style aligned with existing codebase conventions.
constraints: No breaking changes to existing public APIs unless explicitly in scope.
Minimal surface area; avoid exposing internal helpers unless needed.

summary: Add unit tests for all calculator math functions and edge cases.
description: Write tests for add, subtract, multiply, and divide using the project's test runner and conventions. Cover normal cases (positive/negative integers and decimals), zero operands, and division by zero. Ensure tests are deterministic and fast.
objective: Verify correctness and document expected behavior of the calculator, including edge cases.
requirements: Tests for add, subtract, multiply, divide with at least two cases each (e.g. positive, negative, zero).
Explicit test(s) for divide(x, 0) documenting chosen behavior.
Tests run via the project's existing test command and pass in CI.
constraints: Use existing test framework and patterns; no new test runtime unless justified.
